---
title: "Gellan Gum (INS 418)"
slug: gellan-gum-ins-418
category: "Additives & Functional"
category-slug: additives-functional
keywords: ["Gellan gum", "INS 418", "gelling agent", "stabilizer", "thickener", "hydrocolloid", "food additive", "fermentation"]
description: "Gellan gum (INS 418) is a modern hydrocolloid, scientifically discovered in the 1970s and produced via bacterial fermentation of *Sphingomonas elodea*"
---

## History and Sourcing

Gellan gum (INS 418) is a modern hydrocolloid, scientifically discovered in the 1970s and produced via bacterial fermentation of *Sphingomonas elodea*. It has no traditional Indian culinary history or linguistic roots, being a contemporary industrial additive. Its sourcing is entirely through specialized global manufacturers, with India being a significant market for its application in processed foods.

## Home Kitchen Use

Gellan gum is not an ingredient used in traditional Indian home cooking. Indian cuisine historically relies on natural gelling and thickening agents like starches, pectins, or legume-derived gums. Its application is exclusively within industrial food processing, where its specific functional properties are utilized for product development and consistency.

## Industrial Applications

In the modern Indian food industry, gellan gum is extensively used as a gelling agent, stabilizer, and thickener. It forms clear, firm gels at low concentrations and exhibits excellent heat stability. It is crucial for creating textures in dairy alternatives, desserts, jellies, and confectionery. In beverages, it suspends fruit pulp, preventing sedimentation. Its stabilizing properties maintain the integrity and shelf-life of various processed foods, from sauces to ready-to-eat meals.

## Distinctions and Common Confusion

Gellan gum is often confused with other hydrocolloids, but it offers distinct advantages over agents like agar-agar or pectin, including superior clarity, controlled gel strength, and thermal properties. Unlike plant-based gums such as guar or xanthan, which primarily thicken and stabilize, gellan gum forms firm gels. Its microbial origin and unique molecular structure provide specific textural attributes that set it apart from both traditional and other modern gelling agents.
